nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shorelines and sandy sublittoral zones of Lake Malawi; males patrol sandy patches adjacent to rocky outcrops where invertebrates are hunted by sifting the substrate

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep for sand-sifting behaviour
rocky caves and overhangs for territory and refuge
minimal or no rooted plants (will be uprooted); hardy Vallisneria or floating plants tolerated
stacked limestone or holey rock to raise pH buffering

diet.notes: Insectivore/carnivore: in the wild hunts invertebrates buried in sand by detecting water movement with the enlarged lateral-line sensory pits on its head. In aquaria accepts high-quality cichlid pellets, frozen and live bloodworms, daphnia, brine shrimp, and mysis shrimp. Avoid feeder fish and mammalian-protein-based foods (beef heart) which can cause Malawi bloat. Vegetable matter should be minimal; spirulina-enriched foods are generally fine in small amounts.

goodWith: other Aulonocara species (peacock cichlids) of similar size
large Synodontis catfish (e.g., S. petricola) as dither fish
Labidochromis caeruleus (Yellow Lab) — relatively peaceful mbuna
avoidWith: aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Maylandia, Pseudotropheus) — harassment and hybridisation risk
other Aulonocara males without sufficient females and territory (male-on-male aggression)
small tetras or other small community fish (will be eaten or harassed)
other mouthbrooding Aulonocara congeners if hybrid prevention is desired

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Aulonocara nyassae; confirm availability before use.

summary: Brilliantly coloured Lake Malawi peacock cichlid prized for intense male iridescence. A sand-sifting insectivore requiring hard alkaline water, fine sand, and a Malawi community. Taxonomy note: A. nyassae may be a synonym of A. stuartgranti in some treatments; trade fish labelled ‘A. nyassae’ are often generic peacock or hybrid lines.
careNotes: Aulonocara nyassae is an intermediate-level Malawi peacock cichlid that rewards keepers who replicate its rift-lake home. Water chemistry is non-negotiable: maintain pH 7.8–8.6, high GH (10–20 dGH) and KH (10–18 dKH), and temperature 24–28 °C. Hard tap water often suits Lake Malawi species directly; soft-water regions should buffer with crushed coral, limestone rock, or a Malawi salt blend. A fine sand substrate of at least 5 cm is essential — males sift mouthfuls of sand to locate buried invertebrates, a distinctive foraging mode tied to the enlarged sensory pits visible along the lower jaw and cheek. Without sand, this natural behaviour is thwarted. Aquascape with stacked rocks providing caves and visual breaks to reduce aggression, leaving open sandy clearings for foraging. A minimum 200 L tank (120 cm footprint) suits a typical group; larger is strongly preferred when mixing multiple peacock species. Keep one male to three or more females to avoid male harassment of any single female. Males can be territorial toward conspecific males, especially in smaller tanks. Avoid mixing with aggressive mbuna — constant harassment stresses peacocks and suppresses male colouration. Feeding: offer high-protein carnivore cichlid pellets as staple with regular frozen bloodworm, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Avoid high-fat mammalian proteins (beef heart) which promote the bacterial Malawi bloat syndrome. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended to maintain water quality and dilute nitrate, which peacocks are moderately sensitive to. Taxonomy note: A. nyassae was the founding ‘type’ species of the Aulonocara genus (Regan, 1922) but subsequent revisions regard it as either a synonym of A. stuartgranti or as a restricted-range species from the Nkhata Bay area. Fish sold in the trade as ‘A. nyassae’ or ‘Emperor Cichlid’ are often undifferentiated peacock lines or hybrids rather than pure A. nyassae.
breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning is triggered by providing flat open sandy areas; the male establishes a loose territory and courts females with flared finnage and lateral colour displays. The female deposits eggs on the substrate and immediately takes them into her buccal cavity for incubation. Clutch size is typically 12–30 eggs. Incubation lasts approximately 21–28 days at 26 °C, after which the female releases free-swimming fry. She continues to shelter fry in her mouth for the first few days post-release. To protect the brooding female and maximise fry survival, a separate 80–100 L brooding tank is recommended; strip brooding (removing eggs at day 14–16 and tumbling them artificially) is used by breeders to allow more rapid re-cycling of females. Fry accept freshly hatched brine shrimp and finely powdered cichlid fry food from day one. Hybridisation with co-housed Aulonocara congeners occurs readily; keep only one Aulonocara species per tank if line purity matters.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aulonocara nyassae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~15 cm males), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.5, temperature 24–26 °C, hardness 179–447 ppm), native range (Lake Malawi), diet (invertebrates — sand-sifting), breeding mode (maternal mouthbrooder), and taxonomy notes on A. nyassae / A. stuartgranti synonymy debate.

additional.name: FishBase — Aulonocara nyassae
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (males to 15.3 cm TL), depth range (3–25 m), temperature (24–27 °C), native range (endemic to Lake Malawi), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes). Confirms carnivore/invertivore diet and Lake Malawi endemism.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Peacock Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-level care guide for Aulonocara peacock cichlids. Confirmed tank size (minimum 55 gal / ~210 L), water parameters (pH 7.8–8.5, temp 76–82 °F / 24–28 °C), diet (protein-rich carnivore foods), and compatibility with other Malawi Haps/peacocks. Notes on Malawi bloat prevention and tank setup.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Aulonocara species
additional.notes: UK hobbyist/expert feature on peacock cichlids covering A. nyassae taxonomy history (original type species of the genus, Regan 1922), sand-sifting lateral-line sensory behaviour, group composition advice (1 male: 3+ females), and mbuna compatibility warnings. Supported taxonomy note and careNotes content.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum.com — Aulonocara nyassae species profile
additional.notes: Community expert species profile. Corroborated pH 7.8–8.6, minimum tank size 200 L, substrate requirements (fine sand), breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, 12–30 eggs, ~21–28 day incubation), and hybridisation risk with other Aulonocara species.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 12–30 eggs buccally for ~21–28 days. Separate brooding tank recommended. Hybridises readily with other Aulonocara species — keep only one Aulonocara per setup if purity is important.
